mnemonics: Think of a compact space as a 'Finite Information' space. Any infinite set of points huddles together (accumulates), so you can describe its entire structure with a finite number of approximations (the finite subcover).
Heine-Borel says: In Euclidean space (Rⁿ), closed + bounded = compact. Remember 'Closed Bounds' are compact, like a closed, bounded interval [a,b].
keyPoints: A set K is compact if every open cover has a finite subcover.
In metric spaces, compactness is equivalent to being sequentially compact (every sequence has a convergent subsequence).
The Heine-Borel Theorem: A subset of Rⁿ (with the usual metric) is compact if and only if it is closed and bounded.
Continuous images of compact sets are compact.
A continuous function on a compact set is uniformly continuous.
commonMistakes: Assuming 'closed and bounded' implies compact in ANY metric space. This is only true for Rⁿ (by Heine-Borel) and not a general property.
Confusing 'complete' (all Cauchy sequences converge) with 'compact'. A compact set is always complete, but a complete set is not necessarily compact (e.g., R is complete but not compact).
Thinking an infinite set with accumulation points is automatically compact. The set must be closed to include those points (e.g., (0,1] is not compact in R).
